lesson: Rely on sorted()/list.sort() being stable (Timsort) so ties keep input order; sort once by the tiebreaker key to layer a secondary ordering without a tuple key.
For counting sort, iterate the input in reverse when placing elements from the cumulative-count array; forward iteration silently reverses same-key order and breaks stability.
Implement quickselect with Lomuto partition and recurse only into the side containing index k, giving O(n) average time to find the kth smallest without fully sorting.

Use functools.cmp_to_key(compare) with a compare(a,b) that returns sign(int(a+b)-int(b+a)) to sort strings by concatenation order (e.g. largest-number problem) when no single-value key() can express it.

Build LSD radix sort as repeated passes of a stable per-digit bucket pass (list-of-lists by digit, then concatenate); each pass must itself be stable or the final ordering corrupts across digit positions.

Use 3-way (Dutch national flag) partitioning in quicksort to split into <, ==, and > pivot regions and recurse only on the outer two; this keeps duplicate-heavy arrays at O(n log n) instead of degrading toward O(n^2) with 2-way partitioning.

Guard recursive quicksort with a depth_limit (~2*log2(n)) that falls back to heapsort (introsort) once exceeded; without it, a last-element pivot on already-sorted input causes O(n) recursion depth and can raise RecursionError, not just run slow.

Count array inversions in O(n log n) by extending merge sort's merge step: whenever a right-side element is taken before the left runs out, add len(left)-i inversions for all remaining left elements at once.
